questions.q: What is the equation for relativistic momentum?
options: \(p = \gamma m_0 v\), where \(\gamma\) is the Lorentz factor and \(m_0\) is the rest mass
\(p = \dfrac{m_0 v}{c}\)
questions.explanation: Relativistic momentum is \(p = \gamma m_0 v\). At low speeds \(\gamma \approx 1\) and it reduces to Newton's \(p = m_0 v\), which is why Newtonian physics works for everyday speeds.

questions.q: Why can no object with mass ever be accelerated to the speed of light?
options: Because light is simply the universe's speed limit and that is the reason
As \(v \to c\), \(\gamma \to \infty\), so the object's momentum and relativistic mass grow without limit, requiring infinite energy to reach \(c\)
Because friction with space increases at high speed
Because the object would become invisible
questions.explanation: The runaway Lorentz factor is the mechanism: as speed approaches c, \(\gamma\) approaches infinity, so mass and momentum approach infinity, and reaching c would need infinite energy — impossible. Only massless photons travel at c. Simply asserting the speed limit is circular.

questions.q: When a particle already near light speed is pushed harder, where does most of the added energy go?
options: Into increasing its speed beyond c
Into increasing its relativistic mass (inertia), since \(m = \gamma m_0\) grows as \(\gamma\) climbs
It is radiated away as heat
Into shortening its length further
questions.explanation: Near light speed, energy added goes increasingly into mass rather than speed. The relativistic mass \(m = \gamma m_0\) keeps climbing, so the particle gets harder to push and barely gains speed.

questions.q: What does \(E = mc^2\) express, and why does a tiny mass yield enormous energy?
options: Mass and energy are unrelated; the energy comes from chemical bonds
Mass and energy are equivalent forms of the same thing; because the conversion factor \(c^2 \approx 9\times10^{16}\) is enormous, even a minuscule mass yields a huge energy
Energy equals mass times the speed of light (not squared)
Mass can only be converted to energy in stars
questions.explanation: Mass and energy are two forms of one thing, interchangeable at the rate \(c^2\). Since \(c^2\) is about \(9\times10^{16}\), a tiny mass gives a colossal energy. The square is essential — using \(c\) instead of \(c^2\) is a common error.

questions.q: An electron and a positron, each of mass \(9.11\times10^{-31}\,\mathrm{kg}\), annihilate completely. How much energy is released? (\(c^2 = 9.0\times10^{16}\))
questions.explanation: Both masses convert: total mass \(= 2\times9.11\times10^{-31} = 1.82\times10^{-30}\,\mathrm{kg}\). \(E = mc^2 = 1.82\times10^{-30}\times9.0\times10^{16} = 1.64\times10^{-13}\,\mathrm{J}\) (about 1.022 MeV). Using one mass would be off by a factor of 2.

questions.q: The Sun loses mass at \(4.4\times10^{9}\,\mathrm{kg\,s^{-1}}\). What is its power output? (\(c^2 = 9.0\times10^{16}\))
questions.explanation: Power = (mass lost per second) \(\times c^2 = 4.4\times10^{9}\times9.0\times10^{16} = 4.0\times10^{26}\,\mathrm{W}\). The answer is a power because the mass loss was already per second.

questions.q: Which three contexts of mass-to-energy conversion does the episode name (Stars, Smash, Smoke), in decreasing order of efficiency?
options: Combustion, fusion, annihilation
Annihilation (100%), nuclear fusion (small but significant), combustion (negligible)
Fission, fusion, combustion — all equally efficient
Annihilation, combustion, fusion
questions.explanation: Matter–antimatter annihilation converts 100% of mass to energy, fusion in stars converts a small but significant fraction, and ordinary combustion converts a negligible fraction. Same equation, wildly different efficiencies.

questions.q: How do particle accelerators provide evidence for special relativity?
options: They show particles exceeding the speed of light, confirming Newton

Above ~0.1c, a constant force gives ever-decreasing acceleration — momentum and mass rise per the Lorentz factor while speed approaches but never reaches c, contradicting Newton's prediction of unlimited speed
They show that mass is perfectly conserved in collisions
They demonstrate that gamma decreases at high speed
questions.explanation: Newton predicts a constant force gives constant acceleration and unbounded speed. Instead, near c the same force gives tiny speed gains as momentum and relativistic mass balloon, with speed capped below c — exactly as relativity predicts and nothing like Newton.

questions.q: Why do photons travel at exactly the speed of light while massive objects cannot?
options: Photons are pushed by a special force
Photons have zero rest mass, so the infinite-energy barrier (from \(\gamma \to \infty\)) does not apply to them
Photons are smaller than other particles
Photons carry no energy
questions.explanation: Massless particles such as photons (rest mass = 0) are not subject to the runaway \(\gamma\) barrier that makes massive objects need infinite energy. Massless travels at c; massive never quite does — two sides of one coin.

questions.q: What is the correct way to describe a fast object's 'mass increase'?
options: The object physically swells and gets bigger
From an external frame its inertia and momentum increase; in its own rest frame nothing changes and its rest mass is unchanged
The object's rest mass permanently increases
The object's atoms multiply
questions.explanation: Relativistic mass increase is an increase in inertia/momentum measured from an external frame — how hard it is to accelerate from outside. In its own rest frame nothing changes; the object does not balloon in size.
